sentences.text: Yeah.
Yes, but I think before I go to that one, which is part of my life as well, my eldest brother Alex-- he reached the stage-- he reached the age when he has to call up to the army.

And because he was Jewish-- at that time, they never-- they didn't take any more Jews into the army-- but the army formed a sort of a working section, which the Jews who were called to the army, they were-- instead of getting into-- getting into the real army, they put them into this working section.
And so in 1942, he was called in and he left home.
He was stationed in-- in Hungary for a while.
I mean, I think he was in Miskolc, which is a rather large city northwest from Budapest.
I went a couple of time to visit him.
And soon after, they were taken out of the country.
I think they were taken either to Poland or to Romania, you know, when--
No, they didn't wear uniform.
They just wear some sort of a-- some of them wear some army hat.
And they had an armband, a yellow armband, which was representing that they are Jews.
But some had white armband, which represented that they are converted, Jews converted to Christianity.
Yeah-- no, just an armband.
Just a color.
And so we had to start to organize our life.
The life became-- in a way it was good, because Budapest was a nice city, and we really enjoyed it.
To make some sort of a living, money-- money was always sort of quite an important part of life, and especially in Budapest was more difficult, more expensive than.
Whatever father started, I mean, he had no chance to do it, because there was no-- no goods available.
And so I thought I might try to do something.
So I went to the wholesalers, who were dealing into this kitchenware.
And I asked them, I would like to be a-- traditionally, a traveling salesman.
So I got a catalog and I got sort of a price list.
I have-- the little money which we had I took in my pocket.
I got dressed nicely and started to travel.
And I was selling enormous amounts of goods.
I mean, you know, I worked it out in my mind that it's a fortune which I am earning.
Coming back, obviously, nothing except a wooden spoon was available.
And I was very depressed.
You know, it's-- young people have got so easily to come to sort of enthusiasm.
And they see, you know, such a beautiful result of something, and then they cut it off.
And so in my desperation-- because I didn't know how long would our money last, really, the way we were.
So I passed a street, where there were some small artisans doing all sort of things-- panel beater-- not panel beaters, but sheet metal workers, an electrician.
It's somehow in a cellar under the building.
And as I was passing that street-- I think it was [?
Wesselenyi, ?] which was a Jewish part-- I heard some bang bang.
And I looked out, and I see an elderly fellow forming, out of a zinc [INAUDIBLE] and sheet-- sheet, some sort of a dish, some big, big, big container.
So I went down and I ask him, hello, and say-- he was actually-- he was Jewish.
And I thought he came not such a long ago from Poland, because he's got sort of that accent.
And I ask him what he was doing.
I say, a woman from the house ask him to make him a big boiling pot-- big one, galvanized.
Because that's where she boils the-- the washing.
And she says, it's impossible.
We can't get it anywhere.
Well I knew that he can't get it anywhere.
So I'm doing it.
I say, how much you charge for things like that?
I don't know [INAUDIBLE].
I just say hypothetical number, say $10 a pot.
And I look down.
And I see he's got quite a lot of material.
And I said, what's that material for?
He said, I'm repairing gutters and you know, everything.
I said-- I ask him, what do you like to do better, gutters or sitting here and doing this big galvanized pot?
He said, I'd rather do this one.
And how much-- how much-- how many you can produce from that material?
I mean, this is-- the numbers are only hypothetical.
So he said, I can do 20.
Well, how much does it cost?
He said, it costs $10, yeah.
I say, I'll buy the twenty off you, if you do it for me.
I had to prove my genuinity.
I'll will be back in half an hour, and I pay you in advance.
So I went to the wholesale.
And I said, I've got 20 pots if you want.
Oh, 20, 2,000-- bring me anything.
No, at the moment, I've got 20.
Said, bring it to me.
Yes, but it's one hitch.
It cost twelve dollars-- I mean, this number-- and I need the money now and deliver in a week time.
So he was good to [? hear. ?] So he gave me the money.
I went there.
I paid, say, the $200 to the-- to the--
--sheet metal worker.
And I kept $40.
And I went home and I brought $40.
So that gave me an idea, that artisans who are-- like that in Budapest must have been at least 50, 100, or 200.
So I started walking.
I went to each of them.
And in a very short time, there was no roofs repaired, no gutters repaired.
All of them were working for me.
And I made a fortune.
I made to such an extent that I extended the business, that I did not-- they didn't work for me.
I just bought-- they got entitlement to buy those materials.
So I bought that entitlement list, or whatever.
And I bought the whole lot.
And then I bought the material and a factory was doing it for me.
I was flooding.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, oh yeah, anybody.
I mean, I-- people were queueing up.
I had a little office.
There was a Royal-- Hotel Royal.
And there was an espresso there.
I used to go into that one.
And one day I go in-- I was 17 years old.
I go to the girl, which I knew [INAUDIBLE], said, would you like to be my secretary?
And I said, yes.
And what do I have to do?
Nothing, you just take the messages for me.
And I'm here every morning at 8 o'clock, right?
So tell the people to meet me.
And I came nicely dressed, in sort of a-- and I came into that, sat down, and people were queueing up-- wholesalers from all over the country.
They begged me.
They all came to me, try to-- sort of bribe me.
I knew your father, and I bought always from him, everything.
Sort of a-- you know, it's-- I didn't know.
Believe me, it was a dream for me.
And it was a real reality.
So every day, I got home, money like that, and mother put it in a-- in a iron sort of a [INAUDIBLE].
There life has changed completely.
I mean, it was something-- one of my-- one of my happiest time in life, that I could do something to make the misery more comfortable.
Because the misery and the pain, which the brothers and sisters, are [INAUDIBLE]-- gone-- at least physically they should really enjoy it.
I mean, he couldn't.
There was nowhere.
And he couldn't do anything.
Yes, there was no work for--
Pardon?
Yes, I mean, that-- that came into-- I think, either that or later.
I don't know exactly.
But there was ration cards.
There was very short-- shortage in everything.
But when you had money, you had everything.
So I could see their life has changed, my father and mother.
See, my father used to go to cafe and playing cards.
It kept him home.
And hand by hand, they went to the market.
They picked the best apples, the best of everything.
You know, money didn't-- you see, it's-- and there's such a sort of a strong attraction brought between two of them.
I felt that they-- it's they fall in love again.
You know, I mean, at least part of that stress is gone off.
It was really beautiful to see it.
On the weekend, we went into the Varosliget, which was a big park and everything.
There was music-- and started sort of we live a little bit.
You know, it's-- it's changed.
Something real have changed for-- for how long will it last?
I mean, one really didn't know.
So that's what I was doing.
And sort of a-- it was our life has changed, and we lived very well-- And how long?
--financially.
And we had enough money to-- to breach through the very difficult times.
Because whatever it is, money is always important.
Well this was 1942.
And it lasted till 1944.
They were happy.
I mean, I was happy and everybody was happy.
And-- except you know, this pain of--
Yeah.
Now you see, we'd been aware a lot of things, ourselves, in Budapest, like other people didn't.
Because people in Budapest lived there all the time.
They thought, ah, it will pass, will never change.
But we came from that area of Fil'akovo.
And my mother parents-- mother-- my mother was-- my mother had brothers in-- in Slovakia.
And we knew what's happening.
And we knew that until we are not occupied by Germany, it could go.
But once the German occupation comes, the things will change dramatically.
Because we knew about Auschwitz.
People were coming-- you know, sort of smuggling through-- through the Slovakian border.
And they came to Budapest.
There was some relatives from-- from up north.
They brought news, terrible news.
I mean, they knew it and they--
Yes, what-- what's going on.
Exactly.
We knew exactly.
We knew Auschwitz, too.
No, we didn't know about the gasses.
But we knew about Auschwitz, Buchenwald, all these.
They came and they told us.
They know only one thing, that people are taken away and they never return.
So they don't hear from them anything.
So that's why, when-- sort of, we prepared ourselves for the worst to come.
It's 1944. 19-- is that--
No, I did not.
And I never was in a position to-- to think about leaving Europe.
You see, when people left Europe, I was too young.
But my parents, or my father, definitely were thinking of leaving Europe, very strongly.
At one time, he even entertained the idea to go to Brazil.
But I think his heart was always to go to Palestine at that time.
And we could have.
But each time we came-- this question came up, so you know, you talk over everything within the family.
And he told her mother-- you know, she was a widow.
And I would say to him, that's what I want to do.
That's what I go-- it was-- it's things are not good.
It's-- it's terrible.
And this-- we've got to go out of this country.
We knew, always, you know, that something has to be done.
And then her-- she asked, my son, you have to take me with you.
How can you leave me?
And how can you leave Leopold?
And how can you leave-- this is a family.
You just can't go.
Because nobody from our family has ever left abroad, anywhere.
We all lived there for many, many years, without really anybody leaving.
There's no-- we never had anybody in America or anywhere.
So that never came into-- into reality, even if you thought of it.
Because you can't leave your family.
So that-- that's-- that answers your question.
Right.
But then later, even if he would have thought it, we never had a chance anymore.
So in 1944, in 19th of March, the Germans occupied Hungary.
And then we knew what's-- what's the next step.
There was no question in our mind.
Yes, it came overnight.
Suddenly they were there.
I mean, from Austria, to drive into-- to Budapest takes two or three hours.
I mean, this is-- and there's-- and they could go as far as they wanted.
There was not any obstruction.
So we sat together.
And we said, we've got to do something now, save ourselves.
How?
So we worked out the plan.
Father was still there.
And Father had some sort of a business to finalize.
Because he used to represent a firm from Plesivec, which is not far from Fil'akova.
They were manufacturing all sorts of hard ironware, like hammers and-- and then he was in consignment, which he was buying, and wanting to sell.
And I think he was owing some money to them.
So he said he wanted travel there, and just to fix his affair, to pay whatever he-- and that was in the 20th of-- the next day was 20th of March.
He went out to the railway station, which was the [NON-ENGLISH].
Eastern Railway station.
And as he went in, there was Hungarian police, and SS, and everybody standing there.
So why he was in-- I don't know.
I mean, I wasn't there.
He couldn't get out.
So all the Jews were-- had to produce identification cards.
And obvious, on an identification card is written that Jewish.
So there was no question of them.
So men, they all put them aside.
And later, they-- they took them to a detention camp, which is Kistarcsa.
That was close vicinity to Budapest.
I don't know, it was about 15 kilometers or something.
They only took the men?
Pardon?
They only took the men, too, yes.
And we're waiting for father.
I mean, he didn't-- and he didn't travel.
He didn't-- and we heard already.
And we knew that father was, at that time, at the railway station.
You see, news goes fast.
I mean, you know, Budapest is not so large.
And my mother face and everything-- you know, it was terrible.
I just thought, I mean, they will let them out or something.
So we've been waiting for some sort of a sign of the father.
Two days later, a Hungarian elderly policeman, who was a guard in that detention camp-- quite looked quite decent and sort of sympathetic-- he brought a letter from our father.
And the father wrote, and that's what happened.
And he asked, you know, certain things-- for [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and underwear.
And so, you know-- so that policeman became a contact for us.
Of course, I mean, he's doing it for money.
So we had quite a few contact with father.
You see, my mother would send him.
I mean, he wasn't eating any [NON-ENGLISH].
He [? served ?] kosher food and-- and then, two weeks later or something, we got a letter from father-- so much enthusiasm.
Always the policeman brought to us.
And we had to pay the money [INAUDIBLE].
And then he was said, if we would send a large suitcase with the policeman to pack the things, because during that time so many things accumulated, right, because he's coming home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he didn't come home.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So that was first shipment from Hungary went to Auschwitz.
Pardon?
Yes, they all went.
[SIGHS] I had to support my mother.
I'm getting a bit sort of soft now, but I was really strong, all the time.
I never-- never cried, or never a tear came out of my eyes-- just to do something, to save.
[SIGHS] But it wasn't easy to support my mother and ourselves.
Alex was away, yes.
So-- and then the next concern was the sister.
You know, there always-- I mean, she was the only girl.
We had to do something.
We must save her.
And we had enough money.
And it helped at that time.
So we knew a woman who was a similar age to my sister.
And when we're being so squeezed financially, we-- we sublet one of the rooms with a tenant.
And she lived there.
She looked reasonably decent.
So I-- [SIGHS] I ask her to come over to our place.
And I told her that we would like to buy her birth certificate-- all the documents what she has in her name.
And I told her what we want to do.
We'd like to leave Edith on-- in her name.
And her document is not a problem, because she always can get a copy of it.
And she came-- she was in Budapest working.
And she came from East Hungary, very close to Romanian border.
So it was very far.
It was Dombegyhaz or somewhere.
And we agreed on sum.
So I paid her the money and she gave the documents.
And also there was-- saw that there's a document of her mother.
I said, that goes with it, right?
Because I thought, maybe, one day my mother will need it.
So we have the documents.
But we didn't do anything then.
Because we still lived where we are.
But we knew that should any-- should anything needed, Edith can go and live on-- on this assumed name.
Do you remember her name?
Pardon?
Yes, [?
Dome Maria. ?] I mean, it's-- if that means anything to you.
Right.
So then the regulations came.
Always something-- is, you know, every day something.
And the-- the next one was that they designated a site or area where the Jews are allowed to live, which was very close, where the real Jewish section was.
But, I mean, the Gentiles lived there, too-- which was Verseny utca, Kazinczy utca, near the-- near the shul.
And-- and it was up to us.
We had to move where we lived.
It's up to us to-- to accommodate ourselves.
So if you have relatives or something-- so luckily, mother had a really distant relative living in number 4, Verseny utca.
So they accepted us.
They gave us one room.
So we went.
Not we went-- I accept-- yes, then when this came, Edith went away.
And in the couturier shop where she worked, there was two Yugoslav people.
One was a designer.
Actually, they came from Slovenia or somewhere-- worked and so she knew them.
And so they were-- she gave a few things to that fellow.
So she went to that place, that particular day, when those regulations came out.
Later, she went back to the-- to the couturier's, which was a living quarter as well, so she lived there.
But she was out of sight.
And she wasn't anymore what she is, but she was a Gentile girl, right?
And she trusted the people that she worked with?
[INAUDIBLE] she worked for there was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] salon.
It was in [?
Vaci ?] utca.
I mean, if that means a lot to people down in Budapest.
And it was-- I mean, she was the couturiere to a-- a actress, [?
Hanti ?] [?
Hanna. ?] So it was a-- it was a very good address.
And so she worked there.
But they didn't know that she's Jewish.
Because see, I mean, she was sort of a-- she was the same, except different name, right?
And so that was these two Yugoslav, and was a very excellent designer.
And these two Yugoslav were illegally in Budapest as well.
They was connected with the partisan-- Yugoslavian partisan connection.
And I think one of them, who was a typical German looking-- and he spoke German-- I think he was at [GERMAN], out of-- and a communist.
So I think he killed an SS officer there.
And he and his accomplice-- the other one-- they had to leave that area, and they came to Budapest.
Because again, sort of they could-- and this-- so it just, by coincidence, and that was Edith there, and that was another blonde, blue-eyed Jewish girl from Romania.
So there was no doubt about these people, right, that they are Jewish.
Everybody was accepted that they are what they are.
And on the end, they took over this salon, because it was Jewish.
I mean, they took the [NON-ENGLISH] away.
And so they-- they carried on with the business.
So Edith reasonably safe.
And I, myself, that time I still moved in with my-- with mother and Frank, into that house in Verseny utca--
--in one room.
I think it was designated.
It wasn't locked up yet, but obviously their ultimate plan was to create a ghetto there, which it became, on the end, in any case.
So [SIGHS] we were-- we had where to sleep.
We had money.
We could buy things.
And we were restricted of moving because I think you had to have a yellow star and there-- there was a curfew.
You can go only this time of the day out, and that time.
And always-- something always happened.
To be outside was never really safe.
But at the beginning, it was-- it wasn't so tragic, I think, sort of a--
Nobody guarded.
You see, this wasn't guarded yet.
It was only just to live there.
And if you went on the street, and you went on the wrong time, or-- you know, so you got arrested or something.
So if you kept to the regulation, you can sort of go on.
And so there was-- we didn't-- we just being very cluttered together.
But we-- we had everything, really.
No-- it was no problem.
No, we never heard from him.
So my turn in my situation came, that as I was born in 1925, so my age group became could they-- could be called up to the army.
So the same way, and the Gentiles must be called up to the army, but I was called up to this working things.
And I saw that-- that call up list-- whatever.
So I just stayed [INAUDIBLE].
I said, I'm not going voluntarily into a slaughter house-- I mean, never.
Then I decided--
Pardon?
Because 1942, things were--
You know, this was different.
Yes, Alex [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, listen, I'm not going to a slaughter house.
I mean, this is-- on my own.
I mean, if they-- God forbid they catch me, or they drag me out possibly, but not-- not-- not free, willingly.
So I had to change my identity.
Because they had to have some sort of identity card.
So I went to the police station-- not in that Jewish section, but a little bit out of Budapest-- and I told them a story that I am a refugee from east, where the Russians are not very far from them.
And my mother told me-- to save me from communism, she asked me to leave that area and go to Budapest.
And here I am.
So, you know, I haven't got any identification.
I want to get a flat to live in.
I can't get anything, because I have no papers.
And what is your name?
So I told them my name.
My name is [?
Lukasz ?] [?
Janosz. ?] I was born in Dombagyhaz, where my sister-- everything was sort of a copy, except the name.
And I said I was born in 1926, because it's no point to have 1925, because I called into the army.
So I might as well I change my birth year as well.
And being [?
Lukasz ?] [?
Janosz, ?] John Lowy-- you know, everything sort of-- I made it as sort of easy and born there, to remember these things.
And I got it.
I have a perfect, legal identification card.
So I was [?
Lukasz ?] [?
Janosz. ?] And I went and I asked for a-- as a refugee, a place to live.
So I was allocated part of a flat unit, in-- not far from-- from the-- from the Eastern Railway station, I think [?
Zuglo ?] utca or something.
And one part of this was occupied by quite a high position officer in the-- in the railway, with a wife and a-- and a little kid.
And one room, or one part, was mine.
But he used the kitchen and everything was there.
So I was a real Gentile with-- and that flat was sometimes belonged to Jewish people.
You see, when Jewish people went out of these areas, so they're allocating the other.
So I was one now allocated as a Gentile.
And obviously, I had to do something.
So I looked into a newspaper.
And they were advertising for a house boy in a cafe restaurant.
That was still in the inner city, in Budapest, [NON-ENGLISH] coffee house.
So I went there.
And they hired me as a house boy.
The house boy's duties were that I had to lit the boiler for heating in the morning-- it was 6 o'clock in the morning-- prepare everything.
I had to take-- there was a tricycle.
I had to go to the baker to get the bread.
I had to go to the market.
So anything-- odd jobs, everything which was around, I had to do it.
So I had a job.
And-- and also, the money did not really matter to me.
But sort of I lived a life, and not hiding in a corner somewhere.
And I had quite an easy situation, because I was new to Budapest.
You see, we came about 18 months or two years before.
And I wasn't really known.
You see, to detect me, really who I am, it wasn't-- it's-- nobody would-- I mean, those people who knew me, I was safe.
Because most of them were Jewish people, right?
And those people who-- I mean, they could not point me out, that he's a Jewish boy, right.
Did you not look Jewish?
I don't-- I mean, you see-- I never looked-- now that I'm getting older, a little bit.
Because no one had any idea.
I never have any-- anywhere any occasion, except one, which I can relate, that I'm Jewish.
So I went everywhere.
I was accepted.
Finished?
[INAUDIBLE] that I'm genuine.
